title: How to Handle Proration in MRR Calculations (Without Losing Your Mind)
meta_description: Proration breaks MRR reporting in sneaky ways. Here's how to handle mid-cycle plan changes, upgrades, and credits without inflating your real recurring revenue.
h1: The Right Way to Handle Proration in Your MRR Calculations
body_markdown:
The first time I noticed something was off, my MRR had jumped $847 in a single afternoon. No new signups. No big annual deal. Just a Tuesday. I'd shipped a pricing page update the week before, a handful of customers upgraded from the $29 plan to the $99 plan mid-cycle, and Stripe — being helpful — prorated them. My homemade MRR script counted the proration invoices as new recurring revenue. It wasn't. It was a one-time catch-up charge dressed in a subscription invoice's clothes.

That was the day I actually learned what proration does to MRR calculations, and why almost every founder I know has gotten it wrong at least once.

So let's talk about the right way to handle it.

## What proration actually is (and why Stripe makes it confusing)

When a customer on the $29/mo plan upgrades to $99/mo fifteen days into their billing cycle, Stripe doesn't just start charging them $99 next month. It calculates a credit for the unused portion of the $29 plan — roughly $14.50 — and charges them the prorated cost of the $99 plan for the remaining fifteen days, roughly $49.50. Net: a one-time invoice of about $35, then $99/mo going forward.

That one-time $35 is not MRR. It's a one-time adjustment. But if you're pulling invoice data from Stripe's API and summing it up, it looks exactly like a recurring charge, because it lives on the subscription object.

This is where people go wrong. Specifically, three places:

1. Counting proration invoices as new MRR
2. Double-counting the plan change (old plan drops, new plan adds, proration adds again)
3. Ignoring proration credits entirely and overstating revenue

Stripe's own [MRR calculation in the Sigma schema](https://stripe.com/docs/stripe-data) explicitly excludes one-time invoice items, which should tell you something. If Stripe itself strips proration out of MRR, you probably should too.

### The thing nobody tells you

Proration line items on invoices have a specific signature. In the Stripe API, look at `invoice.lines.data[].proration` — it's a boolean. When it's `true`, that line is a proration adjustment, not recurring revenue. Baremetrics filters these out by default. ChartMogul does too. If you're rolling your own reporting (which I did for way too long), you need to filter them out yourself.

I got this wrong for six months. My reported MRR was consistently 4-8% higher than reality, and I only caught it when I tried to reconcile my dashboard against a Baremetrics trial and the numbers were off by almost $2,000.

## The right mental model: MRR is a snapshot, not a sum

Here's the shift that fixed it for me.

Stop thinking about MRR as "the sum of money that came in this month." That's revenue. That's cash. That's useful, but it's not MRR.

MRR is a snapshot of active subscriptions at a point in time, normalized to monthly. Full stop.

If I have 100 customers paying $49/mo and 20 customers paying $490/yr, my MRR at this moment is (100 × $49) + (20 × $490/12) = $4,900 + $816.67 = $5,716.67. It doesn't matter how much money hit my bank account this month. It doesn't matter if someone upgraded on the 3rd or the 28th. MRR is what I'd earn this month if every active subscription just... kept running.

Once you internalize that, proration becomes obvious: it's not part of MRR because it's not recurring. It's a one-time true-up for the gap between what a customer was paying and what they should have been paying.

And upgrades/downgrades? Those do affect MRR — but only the *new* plan amount matters going forward, not the proration invoice.

## How to actually calculate it

Pseudocode for the approach that works, assuming you're pulling from Stripe directly:

```
for each active subscription:
  find the current plan price (the one that will charge next cycle)
  normalize to monthly:
    - monthly plan: price as-is
    - annual plan: price / 12
    - quarterly: price / 3
  add to MRR total

ignore invoices entirely for MRR purposes
ignore one-time charges
ignore proration line items
ignore setup fees
```

The key move is pulling from the `subscriptions` endpoint and walking each subscription's current `items`, not from the `invoices` endpoint. Invoices are a cash-flow view. Subscriptions are a contractual-state view. MRR lives in the second world.

One gotcha: when a customer upgrades mid-cycle, Stripe updates the subscription immediately. So if you pull subscriptions at 11:59am and someone upgrades at 12:01pm, your noon snapshot shows them on the new plan. Good — that's what you want. Your MRR reflects reality as of right now.

But if you're calculating historical MRR (say, what was my MRR on March 15th?), you need subscription *schedules* or a time-series of subscription state changes, because the current subscription object doesn't know what plan someone was on three weeks ago. This is where most DIY dashboards fall apart. Stripe's [subscription schedules feature](https://stripe.com/docs/billing/subscriptions/subscription-schedules), rolled out more broadly in 2019 and expanded in 2022, helps, but historical MRR reconstruction is genuinely hard if you didn't log events from day one.

I still don't love this approach, but the honest answer for historical MRR is: log subscription change events to your own database the day you start the business, or accept that your pre-logging history will be approximate.

## Upgrades, downgrades, and the "expansion MRR" question

Here's where opinions diverge, and I'll plant a flag.

When a $29/mo customer upgrades to $99/mo, your MRR goes up by $70. Everyone agrees on that. The question is: how do you categorize that $70?

Some people (ProfitWell's framework, for instance) call it "expansion MRR" and report it as a separate category from "new MRR." Some people just lump everything into net new MRR. Jason Cohen had a good [2015 post on WP Engine's blog about MRR churn math](https://blog.asmartbear.com/saas-churn-rate.html) that made me think harder about this, even though he was focused on churn, not expansion.

My take: if you're under $50k MRR, you almost certainly don't need to separate expansion from new MRR. You need to know three things — what's my MRR today, what was it 30 days ago, and what's the delta. Splitting that delta into four flavors (new, expansion, contraction, churned) is useful at scale. At $8k MRR with 85 customers, it's over-engineering.

Spend that time talking to customers instead.

## The proration-credit trap

One more thing that bit me, and I've seen bite two other founders in the last year.

When you issue a refund or apply a credit to a customer's account, Stripe can apply it as a "customer balance" that gets used against future invoices. That future invoice looks smaller than the plan price. If your MRR calc is (stupidly) based on the last invoice amount, you'll think that customer downgraded. They didn't. You just gave them a credit.

This is another reason to calculate MRR from subscription state, not invoice amounts. The subscription says they're on the $99 plan. That's the truth. The $67 invoice is just what they paid this month after credits. Those are different numbers and they mean different things.

If you catch yourself writing logic like `if invoice.amount < expected_plan_price: treat_as_downgrade`, stop. You're about to create a dashboard that lies to you during every promotional period and every support-issued credit.

## Just use something that already handles this

I built my own MRR tracker for about eighteen months before admitting the edge cases were eating more time than the actual product. Proration, annual-to-monthly normalization, failed payments vs. actual churn, trial conversions, subscription pauses — each one is a two-hour rabbit hole, and getting any of them wrong means your dashboard is subtly wrong in ways you won't notice until you're making a real decision based on it.

If you're under $50k MRR and tired of logging into Stripe to eyeball things, [Stripe Revenue Tracker](https://example.com) handles the proration filtering I described above out of the box, so the MRR number you see actually reflects your recurring contract value rather than whatever Stripe's dashboard decided to show you today. It's what I wish I'd had in year one.

The meta-lesson from all of this, though, isn't about tooling. It's that MRR is a deliberately narrow metric. It's trying to answer one question — what's my recurring revenue run rate right now? — and every time you let a one-time payment, proration adjustment, or credit sneak into the calculation, you're answering a different question and pretending it's the same one.

Keep the one-time stuff in your cash reports. Keep proration out of MRR. Calculate from subscription state, not from invoices. And for the love of everything, log your subscription events from day one, because future-you will desperately want that history.

Geometric concept: a clean horizontal line representing steady monthly recurring revenue, interrupted by small irregular spikes and dips that break the pattern — representing proration adjustments. Thin isometric grid faintly visible behind. Floating fragmented rectangles suggest invoice slices being separated from the main line. Minimalist, precise, mathematical feel. Soft glow around the main line. Style reminiscent of premium tech magazine illustrations, The Verge or Wired feature artwork. No text, no people, no logos.
